serving.natural_language_portion: One standard starter serving of Oriental Salad in Special Vinegar Dressing is approximately 150 g, as typically plated at a Chinese restaurant such as Din Tai Fung.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed vegetable salad with sesame-vinegar dressing — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for seaweed, bean sprouts, tofu, rice vermicelli, sesame oil, and rice vinegar
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for this dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 30% seaweed (wakame, raw), 25% bean sprouts (mung bean, raw), 20% firm tofu (spicy beancurd), 15% rice vermicelli (cooked), 8% sesame oil dressing, 2% rice vinegar. Values are consistent with published data for similar Chinese cold vegetable starters. Energy cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA vegetable dish analogues.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable dishes and tofu-based foods — analogous estimate based on bean sprouts (GI ~25), tofu (GI ~15), rice vermicelli (GI ~58), and seaweed (GI ~low); weighted composite GI estimated at ~32
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Composite GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) using University of Sydney GI Database values for individual components: mung bean sprouts (~25), firm tofu (~15), rice vermicelli cooked (~58), wakame seaweed (~low, negligible carbohydrate contribution). Weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution per component. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (32 × 7.8 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 3.7 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from vinegar dressing and seasoned beancurd
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (465 mg per 150 g serving), this dish contributes a moderate sodium load and individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium dressing.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~32) and low glycemic load (~3.7 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 3.7 per standard serving, this salad is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a starter, though the rice vermicelli component should be noted as the primary glycemic contributor.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based ingredients, sesame oil providing unsaturated fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and contains predominantly plant-based ingredients with sesame oil providing beneficial monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acids; however, sodium intake should be monitored in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oriental Salad in Special Vinegar Dressing (小菜) is a popular chilled Chinese starter dish composed of julienned seaweed (typically wakame or similar edible seaweed), mung bean sprouts, spicy beancurd (firm tofu seasoned with chilli), and cooked rice vermicelli, all tossed together in a light tangy dressing made with rice vinegar and fragrant sesame oil. The dish is served cold and is a signature appetiser at Din Tai Fung restaurants in Singapore and internationally. The texture is a pleasing contrast of crunchy bean sprouts, silky seaweed, tender vermicelli, and firm tofu, while the flavour profile is tangy, slightly spicy, and nutty from the sesame oil. Nutritionally, this dish is relatively low in calories (approximately 72 kcal per 100 g), moderate in protein (4.2 g per 100 g) from the tofu and seaweed, low in saturated fat, and provides dietary fibre from the vegetable components. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 32 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 310 mg per 100 g due to the seasoned dressing and beancurd, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, and is commonly consumed as a starter in Chinese restaurant settings in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for individual ingredients (seaweed, bean sprouts, tofu, rice vermicelli, sesame oil, rice vinegar). GI is an analogous composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no exact dish-level entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used for individual ingredient anchors. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish contains no hydrogenated fats; all fats are from sesame oil and soy-based tofu. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-based with no animal-derived cholesterol. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal products; seaweed may contain trace pseudovitamin B12 analogues but these are not bioavailable and are not counted. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present. 6. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) using University of Sydney GI Database component values; this is an analogous composite estimate and should be updated if a direct measurement becomes available. 7. Sodium content is moderate and primarily derived from the vinegar dressing and seasoned spicy beancurd; actual restaurant sodium may vary. 8. Serving size of 150 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant starter portion at Din Tai Fung; actual plated weight may vary. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are approximate.

improvement_actions_taken: Cleaned food_name from menu-style '小菜 Oriental Salad in Special Vinegar Dressing' to canonical English 'Oriental Salad in Special Vinegar Dressing'; preserved '小菜' as alias.
Added Chinese simplified and traditional local names, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names to satisfy G6 multilingual requirement for Chinese/Singaporean cuisine.
Constructed component-based nutrient estimates using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for seaweed, bean sprouts, tofu, rice vermicelli, sesame oil, and rice vinegar.
Estimated composite GI of 32 (low) using University of Sydney GI Database component values; documented analogous estimate methodology.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Ensured all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list by adding the missing romanized alias 'Xiǎo cài bàn cù zhī' to the flat aliases list.
Documented all zero values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg) with 'confirmed zero' justification in notes.
Set all ontology match_type values to allowed values only (closest_parent).
Verified no sentinel -1 values are undocumented; all -1 fields replaced with estimated values from component analysis where possible, with notes explaining estimation basis.
